summary: My grandfather, David Dorin, was born in Warsaw, Poland on July 12, 1920. His family was Russian Jewish and had fled to the Republic of Poland, settling in Warsaw to escape the violent Russian pogroms. Warsaw was the starting point of his family's journey before they immigrated to America in 1923. It was a place marked by hardship and the beginning of their search for safety and opportunity.

My grandfather's family fled the violent Russian pogroms that targeted Jews in the early 20th century. These attacks forced them to leave Russia and settle in Poland before eventually immigrating to the United States. The pogroms were a defining event that shaped their migration and search for safety. Without escaping those violent events, their story in America might never have begun.

The American Jewish Joint Distribution Committee played a crucial role in my grandfather's family's immigration story. In 1923, with their help, my grandfather's father was able to purchase tickets for the family to come to America. This organization provided the support that made their journey possible during a time of great uncertainty. Their aid was a lifeline that helped my family start a new life in the United States.

Interestingly, unlike most Jewish immigrants who entered the US through Ellis Island, my grandfather's family arrived in Portland, Maine. This was an unusual entry point for Jewish immigrants at the time. Ellis Island was the common gateway, but our family took a different route, which shaped their early American experience. It was a unique aspect of their immigration story.

My grandfather's family first arrived in Portland, Maine in 1923 after immigrating from Poland. This was their initial landing spot in America before moving on to New York City. Portland was the first place they set foot in the US, marking the beginning of their American journey. It was a brief but important stop in their search for stability and work.

New York City was a major chapter in my grandfather's life. After arriving in Portland, Maine, his family moved to New York City where they struggled to find steady work and a home for three years. Later, David returned to New York City to finish his education, graduating from City College in 1941. The city was both a place of hardship and opportunity for him during his formative years.

For the first nine years of his life, my grandfather lived in Atlanta, Georgia, where he attended elementary and junior high school. Life was challenging for a Jewish immigrant family during the Great Depression era in Atlanta. Despite the difficulties, Atlanta was an important part of his early upbringing before the family moved back to New York City. It was a place where he experienced both cultural challenges and early education.

My grandfather's family lived through the Great Depression while in Atlanta, Georgia, which made their search for work and stability even more difficult. The economic hardships of the 1930s deeply affected their lives as Jewish immigrants. This period shaped their resilience and determination to find better opportunities, eventually leading them back to New York City. The Great Depression was a backdrop to much of their early American experience.

David graduated from the City College of New York in 1941, an institution often called the Harvard of the Proletariat at the time. This education was a pivotal moment in his life, marking the transition from his immigrant childhood to a promising adult future. City College provided him with the foundation to pursue success and eventually serve in the military and business world. It was a place of opportunity and pride for him.

After graduating college, my grandfather enlisted in the United States Army Air Forces as a staff sergeant. His military service during World War II was a significant chapter, where he rose through the ranks and served as a US Intelligence Spy in Japan. This experience not only made him a war veteran but also deepened his commitment to his new country. His time in the Army Air Forces was marked by dedication and adventure.

World War II was a defining period in my grandfather's life. He served in the US Army Air Forces during the war, including intelligence work in Japan. The war ended in 1946, after which he was honorably discharged at Fort Dix, New Jersey. His service during this global conflict shaped his identity as a US citizen and veteran.

After World War II, my grandfather was honorably discharged at Fort Dix, New Jersey in 1946. Fort Dix was the military base where his service officially ended and where he transitioned back to civilian life. This marked the conclusion of his military career and the beginning of his post-war educational and business pursuits. Fort Dix was a significant place in his journey from soldier to civilian.

New Jersey was home to Fort Dix, where my grandfather was discharged from the Army in 1946. Though he didn’t settle in New Jersey, the state was part of his military journey. It represented the end of his wartime service and the gateway to his next chapter in education and business. New Jersey was a brief but important stop in his life story.

After his military service, my grandfather moved to Ohio to continue his education at The Ohio State University. There, he earned his MBA in 1948, which was a crucial step toward his business career. Ohio represented a place of academic achievement and personal growth after years of moving around. It was where he solidified his professional foundation.

My grandfather enrolled at The Ohio State University after World War II and earned his MBA two years later in 1948. This education equipped him with the business knowledge he needed to pursue his career goals. Ohio State was a turning point where he transitioned from military service to becoming a savvy business professional. It was a key milestone in his lifelong pursuit of success.

Chicago was where my grandfather moved after completing his MBA and where he met and married his wife Shirley. Although love brought him to Chicago, the city did not fulfill his business ambitions, prompting another move. Chicago was a significant personal chapter but not the final stop in his search for success. It was part of his journey as a young professional and family man.

Los Angeles became my grandfather’s home after he left Chicago in pursuit of better business opportunities. There, he founded the Dorin Realty Company in 1959 and built a successful career in industrial real estate. LA was where he realized that success didn’t require constant moving; he had created it through his education and experiences. It was the place where his professional dreams truly took shape.

In 1959, my grandfather founded the Dorin Realty Company Inc. in Los Angeles. This company was the culmination of his years of education, military service, and business experience. Through Dorin Realty, he became a successful industrial real estate agent, broker, and property developer. It was the business he built that allowed him to settle and thrive professionally.

My grandfather was a founding member of the Association of Industrial Realtors, joining with other Jewish men across the US. This organization connected him with a network of professionals in his field and helped advance his career in industrial real estate. Being part of this association reflected his leadership and commitment to his profession. It was an important part of his business life after establishing Dorin Realty.
